Imagine a research system built in layers. One layer parses scientific texts into objects, relations, and properties. Trained on Einstein, it can reconstruct how known results were reached: frames, clocks, trains, light signals, simultaneity. Another layer searches for thought experiments “structurally equivalent” to famous ones, especially the train-and-lightning case. A third layer filters outputs using human judgments about what counts as explanatory, elegant, or profound.

The architecture is offered as a reply to the charge of “inductive closure.” The reply says: because the system recombines structures rather than merely extending surface patterns, it may escape the limits of past examples. But the premises force a narrower conclusion. If every filter is trained on human judgments drawn from past explanatory styles, then the whole architecture may still be inductively closed. Its selection pressure is historical. Even if the generator ranges widely, what survives is what resembles explanations humans already recognize.

This matters because success on Einstein does not show capacity for forward discovery. Reverse engineering known discoveries can be excellent precisely because the ontology is already given. The parser knows to look for objects like trains and embankments, relations like coincidence and observation, properties like velocity and simultaneity. Forward discovery is different when the ontology itself must change. Then the crucial move is not rearranging known parts but positing new kinds of parts, or new terms under which old parts are grouped. A system confined by filters trained on past explanatory judgments will tend to reward candidates that fit old ontologies cleanly enough to be legible.

The thought-experiment generator therefore faces a sharp ambiguity. It “hinges” on surfacing something structurally equivalent to Einstein’s case. But structural equivalence can be judged too generously after the fact. Once a discovery is known, many earlier or nearby scenarios can be redescribed as having “the same structure.” That makes retrospective validation easy and prospective generation weak. The system can appear successful by producing cases that interpreters can map back onto Einstein after the answer is already in hand. This is not yet evidence that it can originate the kind of case whose relevance is only visible once the ontology shifts.

Now place the remaining premises into the world this system inhabits. Producing a truce amazed those who fainted when war was abolished. So there exist observers for whom the abolition of war was overwhelming enough to cause collapse, yet a later truce still registers as astonishing. That implies a mismatch between changed reality and inherited expectations: even after a decisive transformation, people continue to respond as if lesser settlements were momentous. In retrospect, the encounter by the brooks made privilege taste like mousse. So a past encounter, viewed later, transformed the felt texture of privilege into something light, sweet, and perhaps airy. Retrospective interpretation alters valence.

Together these details reinforce the same logic: after-the-fact framing can make outcomes seem profound, equivalent, or revelatory in ways not available beforehand. In such a world, amazement and interpretation are unreliable signs of genuine novelty. An architecture filtered through past human explanatory tastes may still mostly produce retrospectively flattering reconstructions rather than forward discoveries that force new ontology.
